title: Rana Plaza: 1,134 Garment Workers Died So You Could Buy a $5 Shirt
description: On April 24, 2013, the Rana Plaza building in Bangladesh collapsed, killing 1,134 garment workers — the deadliest garment industry accident in history. Cracks had appeared the day before. Workers were told to go back in. The building had illegal floors added. The factories inside were suppliers to H&M, Walmart, and Primark. Nothing happened to those companies. #RanaPlaza #FastFashion #Bangladesh #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: On the morning of April 24, 2013, thousands of workers arrived at the Rana Plaza building in Savar, a suburb of Dhaka, Bangladesh. The building housed five garment factories employing over 3,000 workers — mostly women — who made clothing for Western retail brands. The day before, large cracks had appeared in the building's columns and walls. Police had closed the building. The bank and shops on the lower floors had not reopened. The factory owners ordered the workers in. At 8:57 AM, the nine-story building collapsed. One thousand, one hundred and thirty-four people died.
segments.visual: Rana Plaza collapse Bangladesh Savar Dhaka rubble rescue 2013

segments.voice: Rana Plaza was constructed by Sohel Rana, a local political figure connected to the ruling Awami League's youth wing. The building's foundation and structure had been designed for commercial use — shops and offices. Rana had added floors to the building without proper permits, and the garment factories he had allowed to operate on the upper floors had installed heavy industrial machinery — sewing machines, generators, washing equipment — that the structure was not designed to support. The vibration from the machinery, combined with the overloading, stressed a structure that had never been adequate for the purpose it was serving.
segments.visual: building illegal construction additional floors Bangladesh permit

segments.voice: The concrete quality in the building was, investigators found, significantly below specification. Sand from riverbeds — which contains chlorides that accelerate rebar corrosion — was used in the concrete mix. Rebar was undersized. Column spacing was inconsistent with structural requirements. The building had been constructed during a period of explosive growth in Bangladesh's garment sector, in an environment where building inspectors could be accommodated with modest payments and construction speed was more valuable than structural integrity. Rana Plaza was not an outlier — it was representative of its context.
segments.visual: concrete quality rebar test Bangladesh construction deficiency

segments.voice: On April 23, 2013, a worker named Reshma — who would later survive the collapse trapped for 17 days — noticed deep cracks in a pillar on her floor. She told supervisors. Others noticed the cracks too. Workers, alarmed, began to leave. An engineer was called. He inspected the building and recommended it be evacuated immediately. The police agreed and ordered the building closed. The bank and shops on the ground floor closed. But the five garment factories on the upper floors were told by their owners that the building was safe. Workers were told: come in tomorrow or lose your jobs.
segments.visual: building cracks crack pillar inspection engineer warning ignored

segments.voice: The threat to workers' jobs was not empty. Bangladesh's garment industry employs over four million people, predominantly women from rural areas who have come to Dhaka for work. Losing a factory job — for missing a day, for refusing to enter a building — has real and serious economic consequences. The workers who had alternatives did not go in the next morning. Those who felt they had no choice went in. The collapse happened at 8:57 AM — the beginning of the working day, when the factories were filling with the first shift.
segments.visual: garment workers women Bangladesh factory economic necessity

segments.voice: The collapse was, in structural engineering terms, a progressive pancake failure — the same mechanism that destroyed the Sampoong Department Store in Seoul in 1995. When the overloaded columns at lower floors failed, they could no longer support the weight above. Floor slabs pancaked downward in rapid succession. The entire nine floors collapsed into a pile approximately six meters high. People on the upper floors fell the full height of the building. People on lower floors were crushed by the collapsing structure above them. The rescue operation would take weeks.
segments.visual: pancake collapse progressive failure floors stack rescue rubble

segments.voice: The rescue operation was conducted under extremely difficult conditions. The rubble was unstable. Rescuers — many of them volunteer civilians — worked with their hands and improvised tools. The Bangladesh Fire Service, Army, and Navy participated. International search and rescue teams arrived within days. Survivors were pulled from the rubble alive for days after the collapse, in air pockets created by the non-uniform stacking of the collapsed floors. The last survivor — Reshma Begum — was found after 17 days, having survived in a void space sustained by minimal water and food scraps.
segments.visual: rescue operation rubble Bangladesh volunteers survivors days

segments.voice: The clothing brands whose garments were being manufactured in the Rana Plaza factories included some of the most recognizable names in fast fashion: Primark, Walmart, Loblaw, Benetton, Mango. Clothing labels found in the rubble identified the brands. Some brands initially denied knowledge that their garments were being made in the building. Eventually, most acknowledged the connection. The public exposure of their supply chain in the rubble of Rana Plaza was one of the most damaging moments in the history of fashion industry public relations.
segments.visual: clothing labels brands found rubble Rana Plaza fast fashion

segments.voice: The response of the brands was varied and, in many cases, inadequate. Primark moved quickly to commit to compensation. Other brands were slower. The Rana Plaza Arrangement — a trust fund established to compensate victims — set a target of 30 million dollars. It took years to fully fund, and some brands never contributed. The legal liability of Western brands for conditions in their suppliers' factories is a deeply contested area of international law — companies argued that they had no direct employment relationship with the workers and that their responsibility was indirect at best.
segments.visual: brand response compensation fund Rana Plaza victims arrangement

segments.voice: Two major international agreements emerged from Rana Plaza. The Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety — signed by over 200 global brands, primarily European — committed signatory brands to fund factory safety inspections and remediation and to make safety agreements legally binding. The Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety — signed by North American brands — made similar but less binding commitments. The Accord was legally enforceable. The Alliance was not. The difference in legal character reflected a fundamental disagreement about whether brands had a legal obligation to ensure safety in their supply chains.
segments.visual: Bangladesh Accord fire building safety agreement signing brands

segments.voice: Over 3,500 factory inspections were conducted under the Bangladesh Accord and Alliance in the years following Rana Plaza. Thousands of safety violations were identified and remediated. Evacuations and fire safety improvements were implemented in garment factories across Bangladesh. The inspections revealed that the conditions in Rana Plaza — while extreme — were not unique: structural deficiencies, fire hazards, and inadequate exits were found in large numbers of the factories inspected. The scale of the safety deficit in Bangladesh's garment industry was enormous.
segments.visual: factory inspection safety deficiency Bangladesh garment industry

segments.voice: Sohel Rana, the building's owner, was arrested while attempting to flee to India four days after the collapse. He was charged with negligence and violations of construction law. His trial in Bangladesh proceeded slowly through the country's court system. Other building owners, inspectors, and officials connected to the disaster were also charged. The legal process moved at the pace characteristic of Bangladesh's judiciary. As of the early 2020s, convictions had been limited, and the most senior individuals connected to the disaster had not yet been fully held to account.
segments.visual: Sohel Rana arrest Bangladesh owner collapse trial legal

segments.voice: The Rana Plaza disaster changed the public conversation about fast fashion in ways that no previous industry scandal had. The image of clothing being excavated from rubble alongside bodies was visceral and unavoidable. Consumer awareness of supply chain conditions increased. Academic research into fast fashion's human and environmental costs accelerated. Documentary films, books, and journalism about garment industry conditions multiplied. The discourse had existed before Rana Plaza — labor rights groups had been raising concerns for decades. But the scale of the disaster gave it a new urgency.
segments.visual: fast fashion documentary film consumer awareness supply chain

segments.voice: The Bangladesh garment industry — which accounts for approximately 80 percent of the country's export earnings — did not collapse after Rana Plaza. Major brands did not exit Bangladesh in significant numbers. The economic logic of Bangladeshi garment production — extremely low labor costs, high productivity, extensive factory capacity — remained compelling. The changes that occurred were real but incremental: better inspections, improved worker rights, higher minimum wages (though still very low by global standards), and greater transparency in supply chains.
segments.visual: Bangladesh garment industry economy exports brands continued

segments.voice: The women who work in Bangladesh's garment factories are among the most economically important workers in the world — their labor produces a significant fraction of the clothing purchased in wealthy countries. They are also among the most economically vulnerable. The power asymmetry between a Bangladeshi garment worker and a Western clothing brand is nearly absolute. The brand sets prices. The factory owner accepts them. The worker is told to enter a cracked building or lose her job. The cost of clothes in stores reflects all of these negotiations. The cost does not appear on the price tag.
segments.visual: worker power asymmetry brand factory labor rights inequality

segments.voice: The International Labour Organization estimated that a living wage for a Bangladeshi garment worker would require a clothing brand to pay approximately 50 cents more per garment. The retail price of a garment made in Bangladesh is typically many times the factory price. The gap between what a brand pays a factory and what it sells a garment for is large enough that worker welfare improvements are, in economic terms, entirely affordable. The obstacle is not cost. It is the competitive pressure between brands to minimize input costs, and the absence of mandatory legal standards that would level that competition.
segments.visual: wage calculation living wage 50 cents garment cost analysis

segments.voice: The Accord on Fire and Building Safety transitioned to the International Accord in 2021 — a permanent, global version of the original Bangladesh agreement extended to cover garment producing countries beyond Bangladesh. Major brands have signed. The legal enforceability of the Accord — allowing unions to sue brands in their home countries for Accord violations — is a significant precedent in international labor law. It represents the beginning of a legal framework in which Western brands cannot fully outsource their safety obligations along with their manufacturing.
segments.visual: International Accord 2021 global safety garment legal framework

segments.voice: Among the survivors of Rana Plaza are women who lost limbs in the collapse, women who lost coworkers and friends, and women who returned to work in garment factories after their recovery — because the alternative was worse. Their testimony, collected by labor rights organizations and journalists, describes both the horror of the collapse and the structural economic conditions that made it possible. Many have become advocates. Some have become union organizers. Their presence in the labor rights movement is one of the most direct examples of disaster survivors reshaping the systems that endangered them.
segments.visual: survivor advocate union organizer Bangladesh garment women rights

segments.voice: The physical site of Rana Plaza was cleared. A memorial was proposed but as of the early 2020s had not been fully built. The location — a busy suburban area of Savar — has returned to commercial activity. The economic pressures that produced the building and the factories inside it have not fundamentally changed. Bangladesh produces more garments today than it did in 2013. The brands that sourced from Rana Plaza are still selling clothes. The garment workers of Bangladesh are still making them. The question is whether they are doing so in buildings that will hold.
segments.visual: Rana Plaza site Savar Bangladesh memorial future garment industry

segments.voice: One thousand, one hundred and thirty-four people died in Rana Plaza. They were mostly women. They were mostly poor. They had mostly come to Dhaka from rural Bangladesh to earn money for their families. They worked in a building they had been told was safe when they knew it was not, because the cost of refusing was higher than the cost of complying. They are not an abstraction. They are the people whose labor produced the clothes in wardrobes across the United States, Europe, and Australia. The least that owed to them — by the brands, by the consumers, by the system — is that their deaths changed something permanently.
segments.visual: 1134 names memorial tribute Bangladesh women garment workers
